sentences.text: OK.

Well, I will, as soon as I tell you something about when I used to hang around my father's place of work and watch him-- watch him do the work, and some other people who were working in the same area.
So in my spare time, I used to hang around.
And occasionally, they would allow me to-- to help in that.
They had various pieces of equipment there.
And one of them was a forge.
In addition to the machinery they had, they had this forge.
And some of that--
You don't know what a forge is?
That's where you heat steel in order to hammer it into shape.
And so this was an old-fashioned kind of forge, except that it didn't have one of those bellows.
It had a fan that you cranked, to make the-- make the coals really hot, hot enough to-- to heat the steel and be forging temperature.
So they would let me crank that-- that handle.
And I recall one incident, where they had-- one of the men was heating something.
And I got to crank the handle.
And I cranked the handle.
And he had to go somewhere else and do something.
And so I was watching that steel he had in there.
And apparently, it was getting a little bit too hot, and so I slowed things down.
And he didn't show up and I kept slowing things down.
And then finally, when he came back, I heated it up a little more and so it was ready.

And I recall with great pride that he told my father later that that was really remarkable, because if he had his regular helper doing that, this steel would have been burned long before, [LAUGHS] because he had taken so much time.
And if I had really left the-- the temperature as high as it was originally, it would really have burned.
I don't know.
I just lucked out, I guess.
But I can remember that, hearing him tell my father about this remarkable little boy.
He just shook his head and he knew.
[LAUGHS]
Oh, yes.
He used to.
He used to-- not in so many words, but he would let me do things that showed he was proud to see me do that.
I am the youngest, yes.
Oh, no.
If anybody was a favorite, it was my sister.
[LAUGHS]
Not the baby.
No, the sister was.
She was the favorite.
We left Narewka in 1938, and joined my father and started establishing ourselves.
And-- and of course, the war broke out in 1939.
And we had-- he had lost his job.
And we started to have to go out and-- and help the family survive.
Narewka was, at the time, occupied by the Soviets.
When Germany invaded Western Poland, Eastern Poland was occupied by the Soviets, and so that included Narewka.
At the time, we thought that we had made a big mistake in ending up on the German side, and that there was more-- a better-- the situation was better in-- on the Soviet side, at the time.
1,000 people.
Separated the men from the women, drove the men to the edge of the forest.
And the forest was not very far from us.
It was a beautiful forest in-- near Narewka.
And they murdered every man and child in-- near the forest, buried them in a common grave.
The women were then kept in a barn for about three or four days, without food.
And they were taken away and they were murdered at another location.
So as a result, in that one day, every Jewish human being in Narewka died, was murdered, which included my grandparents from both sides, and my uncles and aunts.
Everyone related to us lived in Narewka.
And they disappeared in just this one day.
Of course, at the time we didn't know this.
We were-- 1941, by that time, we were already in a ghetto.
And the laws against the Jews had been more and more restrictive.
And so we didn't know this until the end of the war, in 1945.
We found that out because there were some people who had these connections with Narewka, who worked in this glass factory.
And so when we came back after the war to Poland, my father reconnected with some of his old friends he used to work with.
And they related this incident to him.
We visited that grave, in-- in Poland, in Narewka.
In 1989, I took my family to Poland to visit.
And I took them through all the areas where I was.
And we also, of course, visited the common grave.
The-- the grave was-- had to be dug by the people who were driven down the street, through-- to the-- to the edge of the forest and brutally murdered.
Not one Jewish human being survived that particular action.

I think they have a certain amount of guilt, in the-- in the fact that they're not guilty for the action itself, but for the fact that no one, not one person, had actually survived, has probably something to do with the complicity of the population in Narewka, the non-Jewish population in Narewka at the time.
I'm pretty certain of that.
They were just simply taken over by the non-Jews.
And they proceeded to live in those homes.
No.
I personally never did.
I would never do it.
Because I don't-- I would consider that demeaning, to have to go back there and reclaim that home.
I-- I think that.
It's [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] done.
You know, it's one of those things that's finished.
And I would never-- I would never want to possess that home again, or my grandparents' house.
OK, well--
--the war began.
And restrictive laws began against the Jews with these-- my-- my father lost his job immediately.
He was working in this factory as a tool and die maker and he no longer could work there, because Jews could not work in factories that were non-war-related.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and Jewish ownership was prohibited and businesses were taken away from Jews immediately.
It was 1939.
So I was-- I was going to turn 10 years old that year.
Well, the area was-- where I lived was industrial, in that the glass factory was there and there were several other factories.
And as a matter of fact, my father used to work at different factories because not every factory had a tool and die maker.
So when they had work of that nature, they would ask him to do this for them.
And there was a-- do you want to know about my first job?
[LAUGHS] There was a factory that made soft drinks.
And so I went there to--
No.
Gosh, I don't remember what the name-- I-- I don't know if it had a special name or the bottles were named all these different flavors.
Oh, there was citrus or orange flavor, and some other kind of fruit flavor.
In any case--
I got-- I got a job working in this-- in this factory.
And I thought that was great, because I could drink all the soda I wanted.
But after the first day, I didn't care for much more soda.
[LAUGHS] But I did get-- I did get paid for putting labels on the bottle.
Oh, a small amount of money.
I was able to take a bottle of the soda home, which was a nice treat for the family.
I just brought it home.
Well, my mother would use it to feed the family.
They all did-- all did something-- something to help-- to help out, different places.
My brother was there, for-- for a short time, also doing the same-- similar thing, putting-- putting labels on bottles.
Yes.
Yes.
Well, for one thing, the company was a Jewish-owned company.
I don't recall the name of the owner any longer.
Well, soon after the Germans came, the-- the Jewish star was required.
And in Krakow, we didn't wear a yellow star sewed on.
We had armbands.
The armbands were-- you know, when they first came out, everyone had to wear an armband.
We thought it was pretty silly.
And I was-- I was a little boy.
I never wore one.
It was actually white with a blue star on it.
And the grownups were wearing them.
And it was taken as, well, the war isn't going to go very much longer.
And so if we Jews have to wear armbands, so that's OK.
What difference did that make?
Oh, they were-- they were on-- for sale.
You know, it was just one of those--
Yeah, for sale.
You can buy-- you can buy an armband.
You can even buy-- if you wanted to, you could even buy one that's made out of-- was made out of some kind of plastic, with a elastic on it or something.
No, I don't.
But it's--
Entrepreneurs.
Jewish entrepreneurs, who made the-- made up the bands, the armbands.
The Germans.
It was the-- the-- the governing German [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] committee, that determined that Jews would wear armbands and the Jews could not use public transportation.
And that was one of the reasons for having the armband, so you could be identified.
Not me.
But the-- my older brothers-- my older brothers wore one.
And my father wore an armband.
She wore one.
No.
It was-- as I said, when I-- as I-- in my recollection, it was sort of-- as I think of it back now, it was-- it was not such a bad thing.
It wasn't the worst thing that happened.
We  thought at the time it was the worst thing, but it really wasn't.
You saw where they had little signs on the door, on the way out.
They said, don't forget your armband.
People had no-- couldn't remember they had to put an armband to go outside.
Things-- things got-- of course, getting worse, with the more restrictive laws against the Jews.
And the ghetto was then started.
And it was in the end of 1940.
And the Germans came in 1939.
So by 1940, we were in a ghetto.
This ghetto was actually in the-- on the same side of the Vistula River that we lived, in Podgorze, not-- not very far from our original place where we resided.
This was an edict.
It was plastered all over the walls, in the kiosks.
And everywhere you could imagine, there were these notices.
Well, we went along with everybody else, just-- we received this-- this place, this housing.
And huge numbers of people were just jammed into the area.
And the area of the ghetto was surrounded by brick walls.
We lived in a-- in an apartment house, with many apartments.
It was several stories high.
And we had one of the apartments.
And then, of course, when more people moved in, we took in another couple, to share the apartment with us.
Well, I didn't have any particular things that I-- that I-- that were exactly mine.

But we just took our stuff, our possessions, our furniture, our beds, and things that people own, and loaded it up in one of those wagons, horse-drawn wagons, and hauled it off into the-- into the ghetto.
Not willingly, but there was no choice.
Well--
If-- people who refused were, at worst, caught and brought to the ghetto, or shot.
Yes, uh huh.
Sure.
Well--
I was basically just scared.
And once something like that happens, the-- one's so shocked with the-- with this incident that it's hard to imagine.
And it's a lot like being in a car accident, that-- that one gets spun around, and then-- and then just only later becomes scared and gets the shakes.
It was a man who was, for some reason, escaping from a-- from a German officer.
And he just pulled out his gun and shot him.
All the time.
While we were in the ghetto, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was-- my friend and I went to take a-- a woman to the-- to the hospital.
She was a small woman.
She was an older lady.
And she needed to-- she was very ill.
So he and I got her in a stretcher and we took her to the hospital.
And on the way back, it was curfew time.
We were a little bit--
It was 9 o'clock.
And we were a little bit past that time.
And as we were coming around a corner, there was the guard standing there.
And he was removing his rifle and getting ready to shoot.
And my friend--
Oh, sure.
So he ran one direction.
And I was slightly behind him, so I started running the other direction.
And as I-- and he shot at him first and missed him, then turned and shot at me.
And he missed me, because I had just managed to jam myself into a entryway in another apartment house that was sort of recessed a little bit.
And so I stood there.
And I thought maybe I was shot.
You know, I heard that when you were shot, you can't tell or something.
Anyway, I was just a kid.
I knocked on the door-- only-- all reflex, of course.
And somebody actually opened the door.
And I spent the rest of the night there.
And my parents and my sister and brothers didn't know where I was, or whether I was alive or dead.
It was in 1941.
I must have been about 11 years old.
Well, I never went past that spot again.
I used to go make a big turn to get to where I was going, to-- to avoid that place where the guard stood.
I didn't see it personally, but I saw the results.
Well, they were-- they were in shock when I-- when it happened, and they were so excited.
My mother mostly was to happy to see me and mad that it happened to me, you know, I mean, just two together.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So those are kind of incidences I-- we went through.
And it was constant.
It was-- there was always that-- life in the ghetto was-- the worst part was, for one thing, we didn't have enough food.
The other was this insecurity, constant danger of being shot or getting into some kind of trouble.
Most of the time.
Yeah.
Most of the time.
Well, my father got this-- a job across the street from where he used to work.
There was this factory where he did some work prior to the invasion.
And when the Germans came in, of course they confiscated all these factories and he lost his job.

But that factory across the street was taken over by a German officer, who came to Krakow and came to Poland, as so many did, to seek their fortunes, because it was a-- quite a lucrative thing to do for these Germans to come into a newly-occupied land.
And this particular person was a German officer.
He was a-- in the counterintelligence.
And he served the German army and the German-- the Nazis well, so he deserved the-- to be given this factory.
Oskar Schindler.
He took over a company called Emalia, where they made pots and pans before the war.
And since he heard of my father and that he didn't have a job in the-- in his own place, and so Schindler hired him.
And so as a result, my father ended up to be one of the first Jews that Schindler hired.
Yes, certainly.
Yes, I did.
I met Schindler quite a-- quite a few times.
I ended up on Schindler's list and in Schindler's company, working.

When-- when he was-- then when we were in the ghetto and my father had a job outside of the ghetto, as so many did, it was a great advantage to be able to leave the ghetto to go to work and then come back.
Well, because that way we had a chance to contact non-Jewish people on the outside and able to organize and get some additional food.
And then it was an advantage.
My father would-- used to bring stuff stuffed in his pockets.
Well, you know, we had the exchange, because in the same-- in the factory where he worked as a Jew, there were other working people who were non-Jews.
So there was a certain amount of contact.
So in that way, it was an advantage to have a job.
Well, she had a job in the ghetto.
She was a cleaning lady.
She was cleaning barracks and offices.
She was a hardworking lady.
She could-- it was not hard for her to do this.
She had a-- she had a hard life all along, you know, taking care of  four children, five children.
So--
Between-- between-- between the two of them and what we could do as children to earn something, we were able to survive.
Everybody did something.
Everybody did something.
That was a natural thing.
A normal thing to do was to bring it home.
And my mother would then use it to put food on the table.
But I must mention this, that at the time that we were in the ghetto, we were only-- there were only four children.
The oldest one.
That was Hershel.
He-- he didn't stay around for the Germans to overtake him.
He was a 19-year-old, strong young man.
And he-- you know, during a wartime, when invading armies are about to, you know, invade or about to enter, the people take to the road.
They try to outrun the army.
They try to find a safer place.
Hershel went east as fast as he could.
And he did outrun the German army, up on the Soviet side, where his grandparents lived.
In Narewka.
So during that period of time, between 1939 and 1941, he was living in-- on the Soviet side.
And he also spent some time in the Soviet Union, working.
And then he became ill.
He had an operation-- and this is what we've heard after the war-- and came back to Narewka in 1941, to his grandparents, to recuperate.
In 1941, he was 21.
Well, he met the same fate as the rest of the population [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in 1941, when the Germans invaded and the special troops murdered everyone in town, including him.
So he's in the common grave, in Narewka.
Yep, that's right, and to everyone else that I remember in my mind.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so we were the four of us.
And my sister, my older sister, got-- also got a job that was outside of the ghetto.
She worked in a company called Kabelwerk.
That's a-- a company that did electrical-- made electrical parts, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] various kinds of cables and connections and things like that, and plugs.
And it was a war-related factory, so she got a job and she was able to also get out of the ghetto and come back.
But soon they created a camp around or near the company.
And then she had to go and live there, near the-- in this camp.
It was a labor camp.
Always, yeah.
But the thing was, you know, at the time, these things were happening and no one had any control over this.
There was no control over it.
There was no-one-- there is nothing anyone could do.
And it was the same and even worse to, say, escape from-- from the ghetto, for example.
I could have-- I slipped out of the ghetto many times as a child.
And I didn't look much Jewish, so people didn't pay much attention to me.
But I couldn't stay out there, because I would be discovered, sooner or later, and be turned in.
Already?
